text: Robotic left adrenalectomy for pheochromocytoma.
The patient is a young male with clinical radiographic and biochemical signs of a three point five centimeter left adrenal pheochromocytoma.
The operation has five main components.
The surgeon mobilizes the left colon, pancreas, and spleen, identifies the renal vein, ligates the adrenal vein, and dissects tissues surrounding the left adrenal gland.
This is the only component of the operation which is really non-anatomic.
The surgeon performs dissection in the tissue planes surrounding the adrenal gland, progressively mobilizing the gland from its attachments.

The patient is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position, and the da Vinci XI robotic system is docked from the left side or the back of the patient, preparing for the adrenalectomy procedure.
The robotic arms are equipped with two graspers and a camera on the left side, while the right arm initially holds scissors, which are then replaced with a vessel sealer.
The surgical ports are positioned diagonally, starting midway between the umbilicus and the xiphoid process, either along the midline or slightly to the right for smaller patients.
The surgical ports are distributed down to the left lower quadrant, facilitating access for the robotic arms during the adrenalectomy procedure.
An infraumbilical five millimeter assistance port is positioned for suctioning, aiding in the removal of fluids during the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon mobilizes the left colon using sharp dissection and cautery, carefully separating the colon from surrounding tissues.
This video has been speeded up to save time and to allow for more complete demonstration.
The surgeon identifies the splenial structure just superior to the splenic flexure while mobilizing the colon over Girota's fascia, proceeding from lateral to medial.
The surgeon resects the splenial after dissecting it out, and the specimen is sent for frozen section analysis, confirming its identity as a splenial.
The surgeon continues the dissection medially, progressing from the previously mobilized left colon using sharp dissection and cautery.
The surgeon fully mobilizes the splenic flexure and proximal left colon, completing the dissection and separation of these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects around the left kidney, revealing the left renal vein as it becomes identifiable amidst the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas, likely separating it from surrounding tissues and structures.
The surgeon takes down the lateral attachments to the pancreas and spleen, allowing these organs to be reflected medially.
The surgeon continues dissection in the avascular plane posterior to the pancreas and spleen, achieving complete mobilization of these structures while maintaining a bloodless surgical field.
The surgeon medializes the pancreas, shifting it towards the center of the surgical field, revealing underlying structures for further dissection.
The surgeon reveals a mass in the adrenal gland within the retroperitoneal space, visible after mobilizing surrounding structures.
The surgeon further mobilizes the left colon, revealing the inferior mesenteric vein, which is visibly reflected medially.

The surgeon identifies the left renal vein and dissects along the inferior and medial aspect of the adrenal gland. The pancreas is mobilized from the adrenal gland, revealing the full margins of the enlarged adrenal mass.
The surgeon identifies the splenic vein closely positioned against the posterior aspect of the distal pancreas.
The surgeon identifies the left adrenal vein and performs dissection medial and lateral to the vein. Hemoclips are placed on the vein, allowing it to be safely transected.
The surgeon identifies the renal vein origin of the adrenal vein, highlighting its anatomical connection within the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon identifies an accessory adrenal vein originating superiorly from the phrenic vein, adjacent to the main left adrenal vein, and prepares it for ligation.
The surgeon applies locking hemoclips to secure the left adrenal vein, ensuring hemostasis before transecting the vein.
The surgeon controls and ligates the accessory adrenal vein prior to its transection, ensuring secure management of the vessel.
The surgeon performs dissection along the left renal vein, carefully separating surrounding tissues to expose the vein's course.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the left adrenal vein at its renal vein origin, ensuring hemostasis after ligation.
The surgeon performs dissection around and underneath the adrenal gland, carefully staying just outside the adrenal tissues to fully mobilize the gland along with the pheochromocytoma.
The surgeon addresses the retroperitoneal attachments of the adrenal gland, preparing to further mobilize the gland within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to secure and transect tissues around the adrenal gland, ensuring hemostasis during the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon exposes the left renal artery, which is situated above the left renal vein, revealing the anatomical relationship between these vascular structures.
The surgeon completes the dissection by severing all attachments of the adrenal gland to the retroperitoneum, ensuring the gland is fully mobilized.
The surgeon places the adrenal gland, containing the pheochromocytoma, into an endocatch bag. The bag is then extracted through an expanded surgical port to accommodate the size of the tumor.
